bibliographic_data.title: From High-Level Types to Low-Level Monitors: Synthesizing Verified Runtime Checkers for MAVLink
bibliographic_data.abstract: Standard communication protocols for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), such as MAVLink, lack the capability to enforce the contextual validity of message sequences. Autopilots therefore remain vulnerable to stealthy attacks, where syntactically correct but semantically ill-timed commands induce unsafe states without triggering physical anomaly detectors. Prior work (DATUM) demonstrated that global Refined Multiparty Session Types (RMPSTs) are an effective specification language for centralized MAVLink protocol enforcement, but suffered from two engineering failures: manual proof terms interleaved with protocol definitions, and an OCaml extraction backend whose managed runtime is incompatible with resource-constrained UAV hardware. We present Platum, a framework that addresses both failures with a minimal DSL requiring only the five semantic components of a global session type (sender, receiver, label, payload variable, refinement predicate), whose structural well-formedness conditions are confirmed via reflective decision procedures in Meta-F*. Confirmed specifications are compiled directly into flat, allocation-free C Finite State Machines (FSMs), deployed as centralized proxy monitors at the GCS/UAV communication boundary. Our evaluation demonstrates a 4× reduction in total monitor latency and lower memory overhead compared to DATUM, measured via ArduPilot SITL simulation.

core_content.novelty_claim: Platum addresses the limitations of the DATUM framework by separating specification from well-formedness checking and directly synthesizing C Finite State Machines (FSMs) for deployment as centralized proxy monitors.
core_content.key_methodology: Platum uses a minimal DSL requiring only five semantic components of a global session type, confirmed via reflective decision procedures in Meta-F*, to generate flat, allocation-free C FSMs deployed at the GCS/UAV communication boundary.
outcomes: Lower memory overhead compared to DATUM
core_content.full_text_markdown: Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have evolved from niche operational devices into essential tools, solving complex logistical and surveillance problems. Standard communication protocols like MAVLink lack the capability to enforce contextual validity of message sequences, leaving autopilots vulnerable to stealthy attacks where syntactically correct but semantically ill-timed commands induce unsafe states without triggering physical anomaly detectors. Prior work (DATUM) demonstrated that global Refined Multiparty Session Types (RMPSTs) are effective for MAVLink protocol enforcement but suffered from manual proof terms and an incompatible OCaml extraction backend. Platum separates specification from well-formedness checking, using a minimal DSL to generate flat C FSMs deployed as centralized proxy monitors at the GCS/UAV communication boundary, achieving 4× reduction in monitor latency and lower memory overhead.
